COMPREHENSIVE HEMATOLOGIC MALIGNANCY NEXT-GENERATION SEQUENCING PANEL
Molecular Oncology Laboratory вЂ” Hematopathology Division

Date of Birth:       [redacted вЂ” age 48 at time of specimen collection]
MRN:                 [redacted]
Ordering Physician:  Hematology-Oncology Attending
Indication:          Acute myeloid leukemia вЂ” newly diagnosed; comprehensive molecular profiling at diagnosis
Specimen Type:       Bone marrow aspirate (diagnostic)
Specimen ID:         BMA-[redacted]
Tumor Cellularity (estimated):  Approximately 74% myeloblasts (per concurrent morphology/aspirate differential)
Nucleic Acid Quality: DNA yield adequate; RNA yield adequate; all QC metrics passed

ASSAY METHODOLOGY
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
Assay:               Comprehensive Hematologic Malignancy NGS Panel (468-gene)
Platform:            Illumina NextSeq 550 (paired-end sequencing)
Mean Coverage Depth: 842x (coding exons); hotspot regions в‰Ґ1500x
Minimum Reporting VAF Threshold: 2% (somatic SNVs/indels); 5% (structural variants)
Germline Controls:   Tumor/normal paired analysis not performed; constitutional variants
                     filtered using population databases (gnomAD, 1000 Genomes) and
                     in-house normal panels; germline pathogenicity cannot be fully excluded
                     for variants with high VAF without matched germline sequencing
RNA Fusion Panel:    54 fusion gene pairs (including CBFB-MYH11, RUNX1-RUNX1T1, PML-RARA,
                     KMT2A partners, NUP98 partners, DEK-NUP214, BCR-ABL1) by anchored
                     multiplex PCR / RNA-seq hybrid approach
Copy Number Analysis: Performed by read-depth normalization across panel targets;
                     supplemental cytogenetic data incorporated where available
TMB Calculation:     Somatic mutations per megabase (coding region); low <10 mut/Mb
MSI Analysis:        Fragment-length analysis of 7 microsatellite loci

  targets, consistent with clonal gain. Note: FLT3 (located at 13q12.2), DNMT3A
  (2p23), and other mutated genes are NOT on chromosome 8; trisomy 8 does not
  directly amplify these loci.

  7q/-7:                No detected monosomy 7 or 7q deletion
  5q/-5:                No detected monosomy 5 or 5q deletion
  17p:                  No deletion detected

BIOMARKER SUMMARY

  Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):     4.2 mutations/Mb  [LOW вЂ” threshold <10 mut/Mb]
  Microsatellite Instability (MSI):  STABLE (MSS)

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
INTERPRETATION
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

This comprehensive NGS analysis of the diagnostic bone marrow aspirate identifies a
multi-gene mutational landscape consistent with AML arising from a clonal hematopoiesis
(CHIP) precursor state. The findings are interpreted in the context of the concurrent
diagnosis of AML, NOS (FAB M1 features) with 74% bone marrow myeloblasts and karyotype
46,XX,+8[12]/46,XX[8].

FLT3-ITD (exon 14, 33 bp insertion; VAF 48.2%; allelic ratio 0.91 вЂ” HIGH):
This is the dominant actionable finding. FLT3-ITD is present at high allelic ratio (в‰Ґ0.5
by convention; this assay uses VAF-based allelic ratio = FLT3-ITD VAF / FLT3 wild-type
VAF, estimated at 0.91). High allelic ratio FLT3-ITD is independently associated with
adverse prognosis in AML, particularly in the context of NPM1 wild-type disease. Per
ELN 2022 guidelines, this patient's AML is classified as ADVERSE RISK. FLT3-ITD
constitutes a therapeutic target; FLT3 tyrosine kinase inhibitors (midostaurin in
combination with induction chemotherapy, gilteritinib in relapsed/refractory disease)
are standard-of-care options.

DNMT3A p.R882H (VAF 49.8%):
The R882H hotspot mutation in DNMT3A is the most common DNMT3A mutation in AML and in
clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP). The high VAF (~50%) suggests this
mutation is present in essentially all leukemic cells, consistent with a founder clone
origin, likely predating the FLT3-ITD acquisition. DNMT3A R882H disrupts the methylation
catalytic activity of the DNMT3A homodimer via a dominant-negative mechanism, leading to
focal CpG hypomethylation at specific enhancer regions. Co-occurrence of DNMT3A R882H
with FLT3-ITD (so-called "DNMT3A/FLT3" leukemia) is a recognized biological entity and
is associated with adverse outcomes compared to FLT3-ITD without DNMT3A mutation, though
its independent prognostic impact in a fully adjusted multivariate model remains under
investigation. This mutation is not currently actionable with approved targeted agents,
but may have implications for investigational therapies targeting DNMT3A-mutant AML.

TET2 p.Q1299* (VAF 52.1%):
Loss-of-function nonsense mutation in TET2, a dioxygenase responsible for converting
5-methylcytosine to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine. VAF of 52.1% suggests this is also a
founder-level mutation, possibly co-occurring with DNMT3A R882H in the CHIP precursor
clone (DNMT3A and TET2 co-mutations are among the most common CHIP co-mutation pairs).
TET2 loss-of-function leads to impaired DNA demethylation and aberrant self-renewal of
hematopoietic stem cells. TET2 mutations are not directly targetable with approved agents,
but preclinical data suggest vitamin C (ascorbate) may partially restore TET2 enzyme
activity in TET2-mutant cells; this remains investigational.

WT1 p.R462W (VAF 31.4%):
Missense mutation in Wilms Tumor 1 (WT1), a transcription factor frequently mutated in
AML (~10-15% of cases). The sub-clonal VAF (31.4% vs. DNMT3A/TET2 at ~50%) suggests
WT1 mutation was acquired after the founder DNMT3A/TET2 clone but is present in the
majority of leukemic blasts. WT1 mutations in AML are associated with resistance to
cytarabine-based chemotherapy and have been described as MRD markers given that the
WT1 transcript level (WT1 qPCR) has been used as a disease burden surrogate. Per
concurrent WT1 quantitative PCR data, the WT1 transcript level at diagnosis was elevated;
this may serve as a complementary MRD assessment tool alongside FLT3-ITD PCR.

NRAS p.Q61H (VAF 18.7%):
Activating missense mutation in NRAS at codon 61, a well-characterized oncogenic
hot spot. The subclonal VAF (18.7%) is consistent with a late clonal event or a minor
sub-clone. RAS pathway mutations (NRAS, KRAS, NF1, PTPN11, CBL) are common in AML and
generally arise as secondary mutations contributing to proliferative signaling. Notably,
KRAS mutation is absent (consistent with the known mutual exclusivity between NRAS and
KRAS mutations in most myeloid neoplasms). This subclonal NRAS Q61H mutation could
contribute to therapeutic resistance if the sub-clone is selected under treatment
pressure. MEK/ERK pathway inhibitors may have activity against NRAS-mutant clones but
are not currently standard of care in AML.

KDM6A p.S1025fs (VAF 44.3%):
Frameshift loss-of-function mutation in KDM6A (lysine demethylase 6A, also known as
UTX), an X-linked histone H3K27 demethylase and component of the KMT2D/MLL4 complex.
KDM6A is a recurrent somatic mutation in multiple myeloid malignancies and is more
frequently mutated in females (as the second allele on Xi may already be silenced by
X-inactivation, rendering a single somatic hit sufficient for biallelic functional loss
in some cell contexts). VAF of 44.3% suggests KDM6A mutation is present in a large
clonal fraction. Loss of KDM6A alters H3K27me3 balance and impacts differentiation
block; not currently targetable with approved agents, though EZH2 inhibitors represent
a rational mechanistic approach (investigational in this context).

STAG2 p.E253* (VAF 21.6%):
Nonsense loss-of-function mutation in STAG2, encoding a cohesin complex subunit. STAG2
mutations are enriched in myeloid malignancies and are associated with aberrant DNA
replication fork stability and altered transcriptional regulation of lineage genes.
Subclonal VAF (21.6%) suggests late acquisition. Co-occurrence with DNMT3A and TET2
founders is consistent with a stepwise clonal evolution model. Not currently targetable.

BCOR p.G1522fs (VAF 14.2%):
Frameshift loss-of-function mutation in BCOR, a transcriptional repressor and component
of the PRC1.1 non-canonical polycomb complex. BCOR mutations are seen in ~4-5% of AML
cases and at higher frequency in myelodysplastic syndromes and aplastic anemia, suggesting
a role in early hematopoietic stem/progenitor cell dysregulation. The subclonal VAF
(14.2%) suggests this represents either a late clonal event or a minor competing
sub-clone. Functional impact may contribute to maintenance of leukemic stem cell
self-renewal.

Mutational Architecture and Clonal Hierarchy:
Based on VAF analysis, a plausible clonal architecture is as follows:
  - Founding clone (VAF ~50%): DNMT3A R882H, TET2 Q1299*  в†’ likely CHIP origin
  - Dominant leukemic clone (VAF ~30-50%): FLT3-ITD, KDM6A S1025fs, WT1 R462W
    added to the DNMT3A/TET2 founder background в†’ leukemia-initiating events
  - Subclones (VAF ~15-22%): NRAS Q61H, STAG2 E253*, BCOR G1522fs в†’ secondary
    acquisition consistent with clonal diversification at the time of frank AML

This architecture is consistent with the concept of "CHIP-to-AML" progression in which
a founding DNMT3A/TET2 CHIP clone acquired FLT3-ITD (and KDM6A/WT1 mutations) to
undergo leukemic transformation. The FLT3-ITD allelic ratio of 0.91 is particularly
concerning and reflects either homozygosity or uniparental disomy at the FLT3 locus,
or a high-level enrichment of FLT3-ITD-bearing cells вЂ” all associated with poor outcomes
in standard cytarabine/anthracycline-based therapy without FLT3 inhibitor incorporation.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ACTIONABILITY TIERS AND THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

TIER I вЂ” STRONG CLINICAL ACTIONABILITY (FDA-approved therapy available in this
         tumor type / disease context)

  FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio)
  - Midostaurin (Rydapt): FDA-approved in combination with standard 7+3 induction
    and consolidation chemotherapy for newly diagnosed FLT3-mutated AML (based on
    RATIFY trial, Stone et al., NEJM 2017). This mutation meets criteria for
    midostaurin addition per current NCCN/ELN guidelines.
  - Gilteritinib (Xospata): FDA-approved as monotherapy for relapsed/refractory
    FLT3-mutated AML (ADMIRAL trial). Applicable if disease progresses or recurs.
  - Quizartinib (Vanflyta): FDA-approved (2023) in combination with 7+3 for newly
    diagnosed FLT3-ITD-positive AML and as maintenance after consolidation
    (QuANTUM-First trial). An alternative to midostaurin in the induction setting.
  - Clinical implications: FLT3 inhibitor therapy should be incorporated into front-
    line treatment planning. FLT3 inhibitor resistance mutations (D835, Y842, gatekeeper
    mutations) should be assessed at relapse.

  DNMT3A p.R882H
  - No FDA-approved targeted therapy specific to DNMT3A R882H at this time.
  - This mutation identifies a high-risk AML subset where allogeneic stem cell
    transplantation in first remission is strongly supported by outcome data.
  - Investigational: SY-2101 (oral azacitidine), CC-486, and hypomethylating agents
    may have mechanistic rationale; enrollment in clinical trials may be appropriate.

TIER II вЂ” POTENTIAL ACTIONABILITY (clinical evidence in investigational setting,
          or approved therapy in another tumor type with biological rationale)

  TET2 p.Q1299* (loss of function)
  - Investigational: High-dose ascorbate (vitamin C) to restore TET2 enzymatic
    activity (preclinical rationale; no approved indication).
  - HMAs (azacitidine, decitabine) have preclinical and clinical rationale in
    TET2-mutant myeloid disease; used in maintenance settings.

  WT1 p.R462W
  - WT1-directed immunotherapy (WT1 peptide vaccine, WT1-specific T-cell therapy)
    under investigation in AML; not currently standard of care.
  - Utility as MRD biomarker: WT1 qPCR may complement FLT3-ITD PCR for MRD
    monitoring given subclonal heterogeneity.

  NRAS p.Q61H (subclonal)
  - MEK inhibitors (trametinib) have preclinical activity in NRAS-mutant AML;
    not FDA-approved for AML. Clinical trial consideration if NRAS clone expands
    at relapse.

  KDM6A p.S1025fs
  - EZH2 inhibitors (tazemetostat) have mechanistic rationale in KDM6A-deficient
    malignancies; investigational in AML.

TIER III вЂ” UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE / INFORMATIONAL ONLY

  STAG2 p.E253* вЂ” not currently targetable; informational for risk stratification
  BCOR p.G1522fs вЂ” not currently targetable; informational

TIER IV вЂ” NOT DETECTED, CLINICALLY RELEVANT NEGATIVE RESULTS

  NPM1 wild-type: Absence of NPM1 mutation in the setting of FLT3-ITD upgrades ELN
  risk from intermediate to ADVERSE. NPM1 mutation absence eliminates eligibility
  for NPM1-directed MRD strategies as the sole marker; FLT3-ITD and WT1 PCR serve
  as alternative MRD modalities.

  IDH1/IDH2 wild-type: Patient is not a candidate for ivosidenib (IDH1), enasidenib
  or olutasidenib (IDH2) at this time.

  TP53 wild-type: Absence of TP53 mutation is favorable; etoposide/decitabine
  combinations (common in TP53-mutant AML) are not specifically indicated.

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ELN 2022 RISK CLASSIFICATION BASED ON MOLECULAR FINDINGS
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
  ELN 2022 Risk Category:   ADVERSE
  Primary basis:            FLT3-ITD with HIGH allelic ratio (в‰Ґ0.5), NPM1 wild-type
  Supporting factors:       DNMT3A R882H co-mutation, trisomy 8 (intermediate-risk
                            cytogenetic abnormality, does not override FLT3-ITD
                            adverse designation), complex co-mutation profile
                            (TET2, WT1, KDM6A, STAG2, BCOR, NRAS)
  NOTE: Had NPM1 mutation been present, ELN 2022 would classify FLT3-ITD/NPM1-mut
  as FAVORABLE regardless of allelic ratio. The absence of NPM1 mutation is
  therefore critical to correct risk assignment in this case.

QUALITY METRICS
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ

  Mean on-target coverage:        842x
  % bases at в‰Ґ100x:               98.7%
  % bases at в‰Ґ500x:               91.3%
  Uniformity of coverage (CV):    0.22 (acceptable)
  RNA integrity number (RIN):     7.8 (adequate for fusion analysis)
  Fusion reads mapped:            3.2 million
  All internal QC controls:       PASSED

в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
LIMITATIONS
в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
1. This assay cannot detect all possible mutations in the 468 genes tested; deep
   intronic variants, large structural rearrangements outside fusion panel coverage,
   and variants in genomic regions of low mappability may not be detected.
2. VAF-based clonal hierarchy is an approximation; tumor heterogeneity, aneuploidy,
   and LOH can affect VAF interpretation.
3. Constitutional (germline) variants cannot be fully excluded without matched normal
   tissue sequencing. DNMT3A R882H and TET2 Q1299* at high VAF may represent germline
   predisposition variants; germline testing should be considered per institutional
   policy, particularly given the probable CHIP-origin leukemia scenario.
4. FLT3-ITD allelic ratio by NGS is calculated from VAF data and may differ modestly
   from gel-based PCR allelic ratio methodology; both methods are acceptable per ELN
   2022 guidance.
5. This report reflects findings at the time of specimen analysis. Tumor evolution,
   clonal selection under therapy, and emergence of resistance mutations are expected
   and should be re-evaluated at relapse.

hematopathologist. Results have been communicated to the ordering hematology-oncology
team. Correlation with morphology, immunophenotyping, and conventional cytogenetics
is required for final clinical interpretation.
